properties.name: Pleistocene core (Silver Bluff terrace)
properties.narrative: Sangamonian/Eemian-age (MIS 5e, ~125 ka) barrier-island sand. Exposed and weathered since ~110 ka; soils and oak hammocks developed during 100,000 years of subaerial exposure.

properties.name: Migrating Holocene sandbar (8 ka)
properties.narrative: An offshore sandbar ~6 km east of the Pleistocene core. Sea level rising ~3 mm/yr (Hawkes 2016). Lagoon behind it is filling with biogenic mud that will become Egans Creek marsh. The bar itself is bare or sparsely vegetated.

properties.name: Approaching sandbar (6 ka)
properties.narrative: Sandbar has migrated landward, now ~1.5 km offshore. Sea-level rise is approaching the ~2 mm/yr stabilization threshold (Mariotti 2021). Pioneer dune grasses begin colonizing the highest emergent crests.

properties.name: Holocene wedge at 5.0 ka
properties.narrative: At ~5.0 ka, the Holocene wedge had prograded to ~18% of its modern seaward extent. The east edge of the wedge is a paleo-shoreline mirroring the curvature of Amelia's modern Atlantic coast.

properties.name: Holocene wedge at 4.0 ka
properties.narrative: At ~4.0 ka, the Holocene wedge had prograded to ~32% of its modern seaward extent. The east edge of the wedge is a paleo-shoreline mirroring the curvature of Amelia's modern Atlantic coast.

properties.name: Holocene wedge at 3.0 ka
properties.narrative: At ~3.0 ka, the Holocene wedge had prograded to ~50% of its modern seaward extent. The east edge of the wedge is a paleo-shoreline mirroring the curvature of Amelia's modern Atlantic coast.

properties.name: Holocene wedge at 2.0 ka
properties.narrative: At ~2.0 ka, the Holocene wedge had prograded to ~68% of its modern seaward extent. The east edge of the wedge is a paleo-shoreline mirroring the curvature of Amelia's modern Atlantic coast.

properties.name: Holocene wedge at 1.0 ka
properties.narrative: At ~1.0 ka, the Holocene wedge had prograded to ~82% of its modern seaward extent. The east edge of the wedge is a paleo-shoreline mirroring the curvature of Amelia's modern Atlantic coast.

properties.name: Holocene wedge at 0.5 ka
properties.narrative: At ~0.5 ka, the Holocene wedge had prograded to ~92% of its modern seaward extent. The east edge of the wedge is a paleo-shoreline mirroring the curvature of Amelia's modern Atlantic coast.

properties.name: Holocene wedge at 0.15 ka
properties.narrative: At ~0.1 ka, the Holocene wedge had prograded to ~100% of its modern seaward extent. The east edge of the wedge is a paleo-shoreline mirroring the curvature of Amelia's modern Atlantic coast.

properties.name: Egans Creek marsh
properties.narrative: Mud accumulated by oyster and clam bio-deposition in the back-barrier lagoon. After welding, became a permanent salt-marsh corridor along the Pleistocene/Holocene boundary. Geometry: union of OSM wetland features in the Egans corridor.

properties.name: Beach ridge ~5.0 ka
properties.narrative: Progradational beach ridge formed ~5000 years ago during slow-RSLR Holocene. Ridges separated by 80-150 years on comparable Florida systems.

properties.name: Beach ridge ~4.0 ka
properties.narrative: Progradational beach ridge formed ~4000 years ago during slow-RSLR Holocene. Ridges separated by 80-150 years on comparable Florida systems.

properties.name: Beach ridge ~3.0 ka
properties.narrative: Progradational beach ridge formed ~3000 years ago during slow-RSLR Holocene. Ridges separated by 80-150 years on comparable Florida systems.

properties.name: Beach ridge ~2.0 ka
properties.narrative: Progradational beach ridge formed ~2000 years ago during slow-RSLR Holocene. Ridges separated by 80-150 years on comparable Florida systems.

properties.name: Beach ridge ~1.0 ka
properties.narrative: Progradational beach ridge formed ~1000 years ago during slow-RSLR Holocene. Ridges separated by 80-150 years on comparable Florida systems.

properties.name: LGM shoreline (~130 km east of today)
properties.narrative: At the Last Glacial Maximum (~20 ka), sea level was ~120 m below today. Atlantic shoreline on the outer continental shelf, ~130 km east of present Amelia.

properties.name: South Jetty (St. Marys Entrance)
properties.narrative: South Jetty. Built 1881-1893 by USACE on the north tip of Amelia Island to maintain the St. Marys River navigation channel. Intercepts the natural N→S longshore drift, which carries ~50 truckloads of sand per day in this region. Pre-jetty, that sand crossed Cumberland Sound naturally to supply Amelia's beaches; post-jetty, much of it is jetted to deep water beyond the bar, lost to the littoral system. Direct effects: (a) the north tip of Amelia (Fort Clinch State Park) GAINED a fillet of new land as flood-tide sand was trapped against the jetty; (b) central Amelia LOST what it had gained pre-jetty plus another 50 ft of shoreline between 1893 and 1924, and continued eroding for ~100 years until the Nassau County Shore Protection Project began replicating the natural cross-channel sand flow. Source: Frank Hopf, Dunes Pt 2 (Conserve Nassau).

properties.name: North Jetty (St. Marys Entrance)
properties.narrative: North Jetty. Built 1881-1893 by USACE on the south tip of Cumberland Island, paired with the South Jetty to form the St. Marys River entrance channel. Together the two jetties blocked the natural cross-channel sand flow that used to supply Amelia from Cumberland. The dredged channel between them is maintained today; spoil sand has been periodically placed on Amelia's beaches as part of the Nassau County Shore Protection Project (USACE, Olsen Associates). Source: Frank Hopf, Dunes Pt 2 (Conserve Nassau).
